final_diagnosis: Coronary artery intramural hematoma with contained perforation and pseudo-tamponade physiology

top_10_cases.clinical_similarity: Spontaneous coronary artery rupture with diffuse pericardial fluid without chamber collapse, hemodynamic instability, RCA involvement
Prominent myocardial bridging as clue to hemorrhagic shock during PCI, femoral approach, hypotension during stent deployment
Coronary intramural hematoma post-PCI, rotational atherectomy, LAD stenting
Intramyocardial dissecting hematoma post-PCI, RCA stenting, hemodynamic instability
Post-PCI hemorrhagic shock, transfemoral approach, stent deployment complication
Spontaneous coronary artery dissection causing hemodynamic collapse, no effusion
Spontaneous coronary dissection with hypotension, post-PCI presentation
Acute de novo multivessel spontaneous coronary artery dissection
Spontaneous coronary artery dissection in male with FMD
Giant coronary artery aneurysm masquerading as ventricular mass with hypotension

primary_diagnosis.name: Coronary artery intramural hematoma with contained perforation
primary_diagnosis.supporting_evidence: Multiple cases demonstrate post-PCI intramural hematoma formation causing tamponade-like physiology without frank effusion, particularly with femoral access and complex lesions

pericardial_tamponade.supporting_evidence: Echo explicitly shows no pericardial effusion; this is pseudo-tamponade from intramyocardial blood accumulation, not pericardial blood

drug_induced_hypotension.supporting_evidence: No recent medication changes; hypotension refractory to fluids and vasopressors suggests mechanical cause, not pharmacologic

cardiac_analysis.domain_conclusion: The cardiac domain provides strong evidence for intramural hematoma with contained coronary perforation. Key cases demonstrate similar post-PCI hypotension patterns where intramural blood accumulation creates tamponade-like physiology without pericardial effusion. The RCA CTO setting with hemoglobin drop strongly supports this diagnosis.

top_10_cases.clinical_similarity: Hemorrhagic shock during PCI procedure, femoral access site, rotational atherectomy complication
Post-PCI hemorrhagic shock with intramural hematoma, access site bleeding considered
Subclavian artery stenosis causing refractory hypotension, mimics tamponade physiology
Superior mesenteric artery pseudoaneurysm rupture with contained hemorrhage
Coronary artery aneurysm with hemodynamic collapse
Leiomyosarcoma of IVC with obstructive shock

primary_diagnosis.supporting_evidence: Vascular cases confirm PCI-related hemorrhagic complications can occur without overt external bleeding, supporting contained rupture mechanism

vascular_analysis.domain_conclusion: The vascular domain supports contained vascular injury as a mechanism for hemodynamic collapse. Cases of refractory hypotension from contained hemorrhage provide analogies for the pseudo-tamponade physiology observed.

top_10_cases.clinical_similarity: Azathioprine hypersensitivity syndrome with distributive shock requiring vasopressors
Refractory multivessel coronary spasm with hypereosinophilic syndrome causing hemodynamic instability
Mast cell activation syndrome with hypotension and systemic response

primary_diagnosis.supporting_evidence: Systemic cases of refractory shock provide differential considerations but lack the procedural context and specific findings (hemoglobin drop, tamponade physiology) seen in this case

systemic_analysis.domain_conclusion: The systemic domain is the weakest contributor. While cases of refractory shock exist, they do not match the specific procedural context and localized cardiac findings of this case.

supporting_cases.relevance: Directly analogous - post-PCI hematoma causing tamponade-like physiology without pericardial effusion
supporting_cases.excerpt: Coronary angiography via a right femoral approach showed an 80% stenosis of the mid LAD... During stent deployment, his blood pressure fell dramatically... intramural hematoma was identified

supporting_cases.relevance: Femoral access site hemorrhage presenting with tamponade physiology during PCI
supporting_cases.excerpt: A 73-year-old man with stable angina pectoris was admitted for percutaneous coronary intervention of the proximal LAD... transfemoral approach was performed... During stent deployment, his blood pressure fell

supporting_cases.relevance: Post-PCI intramyocardial hematoma with tamponade-like presentation
supporting_cases.excerpt: A 77-year-old man with coronary artery disease presented with persistent chest pain... coronary angiography showed stenosis in the RCA treated with drug-eluting stent implantation... intramyocardial dissecting haematoma

supporting_cases.relevance: Coronary rupture with pericardial fluid but without chamber collapse - tamponade-like physiology
supporting_cases.excerpt: A 62-year-old woman on maintenance dialysis... presented with chest discomfort... ECG showed atrial fibrillation... diffuse mild pericardial fluid without chamber collapse... troponin elevated... coronary rupture

supporting_cases.relevance: Spontaneous coronary dissection causing hemodynamic instability without pericardial effusion
supporting_cases.excerpt: A 54-year-old woman presented with recurrent episodes of chest pain... coronary angiography showed patent stent and new spontaneous dissection of the distal RCA... hypotensive episodes

primary_diagnosis.name: Coronary artery intramural hematoma with contained perforation
primary_diagnosis.cardiac_domain_support: Strong - 5 cases with direct post-PCI intramural hematoma or contained rupture
primary_diagnosis.vascular_domain_support: Moderate - 2 cases support contained vascular injury mechanism
primary_diagnosis.systemic_domain_support: Weak - no systemic disease mimics this specific presentation

pericardial_tamponade.vascular_domain_support: No supporting cases - tamponade requires pericardial collection
pericardial_tamponade.systemic_domain_support: No relevance to systemic disease patterns

drug_induced_hypotension.cardiac_domain_support: No medication changes documented; refractory to fluids/vasopressors argues against
drug_induced_hypotension.vascular_domain_support: No vascular drug reaction cases relevant
drug_induced_hypotension.systemic_domain_support: Limited - mast cell activation and anaphylaxis cases present but lack PCI context

most_discriminating_case.title: Coronary artery intramural hematoma, a rare complication of percutaneous coronary intervention
discriminatory_features: Absence of pericardial effusion on imaging

excluded_diagnoses.exclusion_reason: Echocardiography demonstrates no pericardial effusion; tamponade physiology is pseudo-tamponade from intramyocardial blood accumulation
Absence of pericardial effusion argues against free rupture; contained perforation with intramural hematoma more consistent with findings
Physical examination reveals no groin hematoma or bleeding; hemodynamic collapse pattern more consistent with cardiac mechanism
Hypotension refractory to fluids and vasopressors; temporal relationship to procedure completion; vagal reactions typically respond to atropine and fluids
No allergic symptoms (urticaria, bronchospasm); no exposure to new allergens; hemodynamic pattern inconsistent with allergic reaction

diagnostic_approach: Cross-domain differential elimination focused on distinguishing true pericardial tamponade from pseudo-tamponade, and free coronary perforation from contained rupture with intramural hematoma. Cardiac domain cases provided direct evidence for intramural hematoma as the unifying diagnosis.
